input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin under study is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware architecture comprises lower‑level and upper‑level chassis sections together with dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception sensors for object detection and an on‑board ECU). The vehicle operates in a laboratory or test‑track environment where adaptive cruise control (ACC) functionality is exercised. The on‑board ECU is resource‑constrained, making the system a prime candidate for off‑loading compute‑intensive ACC algorithms to an external digital twin, and the modular chassis and sensor suite provide the necessary observability for high‑fidelity twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three tightly integrated services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) that streams real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, while also supporting data logging, report generation, and visualization for operators and developers; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed computation) is executed on the DT (typically in cloud or edge resources) and the resulting desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation, relieving the on‑board ECU; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set target speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands, requiring low end‑to‑end latency communication and safety‑critical handling. These services target two user classes: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting and data‑driven analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation leverages NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 for GPU‑accelerated processing, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware and communication backbone, application code written in Python 3.8 and C++, and model development in MATLAB/Simulink. The system is engineered according to the ISO 23247‑1:2021 Digital Twin framework, employing its Reference Architecture (ISO 23247 RA). A custom ROS2 package embodies the DT entity, providing low‑latency vehicle‑DT communication and integration with the user interface.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between physical and digital components with an accuracy of 100 ms (the maximum allowed by hardware), which is the target temporal granularity for all off‑loading and control services; no stricter sub‑100 ms synchronization is provided.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin is organised into three hierarchical entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity – each instantiated as a separate ROS2 package on distinct hardware computing resources; the design does not describe multiple parallel twin instances or a centralized/decentralised split, but the entity‑based decomposition provides a clear ownership hierarchy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture: each functional entity (FE) is implemented as one or more ROS2 nodes packaged together; entities are deployed on different compute nodes, enabling distributed execution; communication between FEs uses ROS2 DDS, and the overall system is coordinated through the entity‑based model that maps software components to physical resources.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is guided by the ISO 23247 framework; FEs may be realized with OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as an example; the demonstrator is published on GitHub (https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin) and a YouTube video, implying possible external toolchain integration; no specific enterprise data‑exchange protocols are documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT’s computational models are realized as ROS‑2 packages (functional entities) that implement point‑cloud clustering and tracking algorithms; these models consume sensor data from the Physical Twin Entity and output processed information stored by a dedicated Data Storage component. Data management is organized within the Digital Twin Entity, with data collection and device control functionality handled by separate functional entities. Model relationships are expressed via ROS‑2 topics connecting the sensor‑ingestion nodes, preprocessing nodes, and the clustering/tracking nodes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is realized within the Physical Twin Entity through ROS‑2 nodes that issue commands to a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (driving the motor) and a steering servo. The Control Panel FE in the User Interface sends desired speed and steering set‑points, which are transformed by the Actuation FE into motor voltage commands and servo pulse widths. Remote control is achieved via ROS‑2 topics, and actuation ranges are limited by the hardware specifications of the motor and servo (not detailed in the documents). Safety constraints are enforced by the 100 ms clock‑synchronisation requirement and by limiting command issuance to the synchronized control loop.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack comprises a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR and an Intel Deepsense D435i depth camera, both instantiated in the Physical Twin Entity. The LiDAR provides 2‑D distance measurements used to generate point clouds, while the depth camera supplies RGB‑D data. These sensors are accessed by ROS‑2 nodes that publish raw point‑cloud streams for downstream processing. Specific sampling frequencies, data rates, and accuracy figures are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity and validation depend on a shared notion of time across devices; the system requires clock synchronization with ≤100 ms accuracy to satisfy safety‑critical vehicular constraints. This timing requirement is imposed on both data acquisition and actuation events. The architecture’s deviation from the ISO 23247 reference (e.g., introduction of a Physical Twin Entity) is noted as potentially affecting ownership and engineering processes, implying a need for verification of entity boundaries.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering servo positions, and overall vehicle state) is captured by the Data Pre‑Processing Front‑End (FE) which includes a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component that converts raw LiDAR points into tracked objects. The processed streams are then handed to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE, which serialises the data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Communication from the physical twin to the digital twin entities occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP as the transport protocol. Inter‑component communication inside the DTS uses a ROS 2 middleware network. The documents do not specify transmission frequencies, explicit event triggers, or data‑quality assurance mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin generates control commands for the vehicle, including desired speed set‑points, ACC enable/disable signals, and emergency‑brake commands, which are sent back to the physical twin over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. These commands are received by the Actuation Front‑End on the vehicle, which drives the motor and steering servo actuators to realize the commanded actions. Validation, authentication, conflict resolution, and explicit acknowledgement of command execution are not described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Within the DTS, software components communicate via a ROS 2 network. The bridge between the digital twin and the physical twin uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP transport. The system is designed to support low end‑to‑end latency communication, although specific bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocol details (e.g., TLS, VPN) are not given.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT services can be off‑loaded to external compute resources, exemplified by deployment in the cloud to relieve the resource‑constrained ECU. The documents mention cloud‑based compute but do not detail the hosting platform specifics, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development process follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture. Key software components are first identified (Table I) and then mapped to Functional Entities (FEs). Each FE is realized as an independent ROS 2 package; a package may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s functionality. The system is decomposed into three entities: the User Entity (hosting the User Interface FE, split into a 3D Visualization component and a Control Panel component), the Digital Twin Entity (hosting data‑management and control mechanisms), and the Physical Twin Entity (hosting data pre‑processing, actuation, and generic automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering & tracking). Each entity is deployed on separate hardware, reflecting ownership of resources and enabling modular evolution. The process emphasizes alignment with the ISO 23247 entity model rather than prescribing specific QA, testing, or version‑control practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is delivered through the User Interface FE, which provides a 3D Visualization component for real‑time monitoring and a Control Panel for issuing commands to the Physical Twin Entity. The Digital Twin Entity supplies processed sensor data and control actions, while the Physical Twin Entity performs analytics such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking to generate situational insights. These insights are presented in a human‑interpretable format within the visualization, enabling operators to make informed control decisions. Confidence in recommendations is implicit in the deterministic nature of the ROS 2‑based processing pipeline, though explicit confidence metrics are not described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built in compliance with ISO 23247 (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing). It adopts the standard’s entity‑based structure—Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—and extends it with a dedicated Physical Twin Entity. Implementation uses ROS 2 packages, which can interoperate with OPC Unified Architecture for device coordination. Clock synchronization is required to meet safety‑critical vehicular standards demanding ≤100 ms accuracy, aligning the twin with industry timing specifications.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The digital twin is required to respect the safety‑critical nature of the vehicle system and to comply with the safety standards referenced in the documents, specifically ISO 15622 (Adaptive Cruise Control performance requirements) and ISO 26262‑1 (functional safety for road vehicles). These references indicate that the twin must be designed and operated in accordance with the safety requirements and verification procedures defined in those standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the details of the physical twin found in Section III.B, including its origin as a '1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle' retrofitted from a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle, the specific hardware architecture comprising 'lower level chassis' and 'upper level chassis', and the inclusion of 'autonomy components' like sensors and an ECU. It also correctly synthesizes the motivation for offloading computation from the 'resource-constrained electronic control units' mentioned in Section III.A.

The extraction aligns perfectly with Section III.A, which explicitly defines the three services: 'Data Monitoring' (UI, vehicle state, projected path, object tracks, reports), 'Computation Offloading' (ACC algorithm execution on DT/Cloud, sending desired speed back), and 'Remote Management' (enable/disable ACC, set speed, emergency brake). It also correctly identifies the user classes: 'operators and developers'.

The description accurately cites NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, ROS2, Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink as tools, along with ISO 23247-1:2021 for the framework. It notes the custom ROS2 package implementation for the DT entity and communication. Quotes: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8 ..., C++ ..., and MATLAB/Simulink', 'ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework ... Part 2 provides a detailed, layered RA', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package'.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific synchronization requirement and its justification. It correctly states the 100 ms accuracy requirement and attributes it to hardware limitations, directly mirroring the source text's requirements and rationale.

Correctly identifies the three hierarchical entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) and their deployment on distinct hardware resources. It accurately describes the implementation using ROS2 packages and nodes, capturing the decomposition structure defined in the case study.

Accurately describes the orchestration based on ISO 23247, the mapping of Functional Entities to ROS2 packages/nodes, and the distributed execution across hardware. However, it claims communication uses 'ROS2 DDS', which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided text (the text only mentions 'ROS2 network' and cuts off at 'using U').

Correctly cites OPC UA as an example implementation possibility from the text and accurately notes the public availability of the source code and demonstration on GitHub and YouTube. It correctly observes that no specific enterprise protocols are mandated in the implementation details provided.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design

and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement

each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated

components that implement data management and control

_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from

in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin

automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_

detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

It does not contain any information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the assessment that this is 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction correctly identifies the safety-critical nature of the system and references specific ISO standards mentioned in the text. The source states the DT must operate 'while respecting the safety-critical nature of the system' and explicitly cites 'ISO 15622' as a requirement for ACC implementation ('The system shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622'). However, the source mentions ISO 26262-1 generally in the context of DTs being used in safety-critical domains ('complex cyber-physical systems... safety-critical...'), but does not explicitly link ISO 26262-1 compliance requirements to this specific DT design in the requirements section. The extraction is accurate regarding the safety-critical context and the ISO 15622 reference, but the ISO 26262-1 reference is an inference from the general background section rather than a specific requirement for this case study's DT.
